title: Cost of Capital & WACC
simple_html:
Every dollar a company invests has to come from somewhere — lenders, who expect interest, or shareholders, who expect a return on their equity. The cost of capital is simply the blended rate the firm must earn on its projects just to keep all those money-providers happy. If a project earns less than this rate, it destroys value; if it earns more, it creates value.

Why it matters

The cost of capital is the hurdle rate used in capital budgeting. When you calculate Net Present Value (NPV), the discount rate you use is almost always the company's WACC — the Weighted Average Cost of Capital. Get the WACC wrong and every investment decision built on it is wrong too.

The core idea: WACC

WACC mixes the cost of debt and the cost of equity in proportion to how much of each the firm uses:

WACC = (E/V × Re) + (D/V × Rd × (1 − Tax))

Here E is the market value of equity, D is debt, V = E + D, Re is the return shareholders require, and Rd is the interest rate on debt. Debt is multiplied by (1 − Tax) because interest is tax-deductible — a feature called the tax shield that makes debt cheaper than it looks.

Key takeaways

  • Cost of capital = the minimum return a firm must earn to satisfy its investors.
  • WACC blends debt and equity costs by their market-value weights.
  • Debt is usually cheaper than equity (it's safer for the provider) and tax-deductible.
  • WACC is the standard discount rate for NPV and project valuation.
  • A project should be accepted only if its expected return beats the WACC.
intermediate_html:
The cost of capital is the return a firm must generate to compensate everyone who funds it. Because firms use a mix of financing, we compute a single blended figure — the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) — and use it as the hurdle rate for investment decisions.

The WACC formula

WACC = (E/V)Re + (D/V)Rd(1 − Tc)

where E = market value of equity, D = market value of debt, V = E + D, Re = cost of equity, Rd = pre-tax cost of debt, and Tc = corporate tax rate. Weights should be based on market values, not book values, because they reflect what investors actually have at stake today.

Estimating the cost of equity

The most common method is the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM):

Re = Rf + β(Rm − Rf)

Rf is the risk-free rate (e.g., a government bond yield), β measures the stock's sensitivity to market movements, and (Rm − Rf) is the equity market risk premium. A stock with β = 1.2 is 20% more volatile than the market and so demands a higher return.

Estimating the cost of debt

The cost of debt is the yield to maturity on the firm's bonds or the interest rate it pays on new borrowing. Because interest is tax-deductible, the after-tax cost is Rd(1 − Tc). This tax shield is the reason debt financing lowers WACC — up to a point.

Worked example

Suppose a firm has:

  • Equity worth $600m, debt worth $400m, so V = $1,000m (E/V = 0.6, D/V = 0.4).
  • Cost of equity Re = 12% (from CAPM).
  • Pre-tax cost of debt Rd = 6%; tax rate Tc = 25%.

After-tax cost of debt = 6% × (1 − 0.25) = 4.5%.

WACC = (0.6 × 12%) + (0.4 × 4.5%) = 7.2% + 1.8% = 9.0%.

Any new project of similar risk must return more than 9% to add value.

How to apply it

  • Use the right discount rate. Discount a project's free cash flows at WACC only if the project has the same risk as the firm overall; otherwise adjust the rate up or down for risk.
  • Use market-value weights and current costs, not historical book figures.
  • Match the tax shield correctly — only the cost of debt gets the (1 − Tc) adjustment.
  • Watch the capital structure. Changing the debt/equity mix changes the weights and the individual costs (more debt raises both Re and Rd as risk rises).

Common pitfalls

Don't use book-value weights, don't forget the tax shield, and don't apply the company-wide WACC to a project that is far riskier or safer than the firm's existing business — a classic error that leads firms to accept bad risky projects and reject good safe ones.
full_html:
The cost of capital is the expected rate of return that capital providers — debtholders and equity investors — require for supplying funds to a firm, given the risk of those funds. Operationally it is the firm's opportunity cost of capital and, in most applications, it is summarized by the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC). WACC links a firm's financing decisions (Lesson 7, Capital Structure) to its investment decisions (Lesson 4, Capital Budgeting), because it is the discount rate that converts a project's future cash flows into present value.

Conceptual foundation

The cost of capital rests on the principle that capital is never free: investors could deploy their money elsewhere at comparable risk. The required return therefore reflects the time value of money (Lesson 2) plus a risk premium. Modigliani and Miller's seminal propositions (Modigliani & Miller, 1958; 1963) established that, in perfect markets, a firm's overall cost of capital is determined by the risk of its assets, not by how those assets are financed — although taxes (MM, 1963) reintroduce a financing effect through the interest tax shield.

The WACC formula

WACC = (E/V)Re + (D/V)Rd(1 − Tc)

with E and D the market values of equity and debt, V = E + D, Re the cost of equity, Rd the pre-tax cost of debt, and Tc the marginal corporate tax rate. If preferred stock is present, a third term (P/V)Rp is added. Three rules govern correct use: (1) use market values for the weights; (2) use current, forward-looking costs, not historical averages; (3) the tax adjustment applies only to debt, because only interest is tax-deductible.

Estimating the cost of equity

The dominant approach is the Capital Asset Pricing Model (Sharpe, 1964; Lintner, 1965), covered in depth in Lesson 5:

Re = Rf + β(Rm − Rf)

The risk-free rate Rf is typically a long-term government bond yield; β is estimated by regressing the stock's returns on market returns; the equity risk premium (Rm − Rf) is estimated from long-run historical data or implied from current prices. Alternatives include the Dividend Discount (Gordon Growth) Model, Re = D₁/P₀ + g (Gordon, 1962), and multi-factor models such as Fama–French (Fama & French, 1993), which add size and value factors to address CAPM's empirical shortcomings.

Estimating the cost of debt

The cost of debt is the yield to maturity on the firm's outstanding bonds or the marginal rate on new borrowing of comparable maturity and seniority — not the coupon rate and not the historical average rate. For firms without traded debt, a synthetic rating maps interest-coverage ratios to a credit spread. The after-tax cost, Rd(1 − Tc), captures the tax shield: each dollar of interest reduces taxable income, so the government effectively subsidizes debt financing.

Worked example

A firm has market-value equity of $600m and debt of $400m, so V = $1,000m. From CAPM with Rf = 4%, β = 1.3, and a risk premium of 6.15%, Re = 4% + 1.3 × 6.15% = 12.0%. Its bonds yield 6% and the tax rate is 25%, so the after-tax cost of debt is 4.5%. Then:

WACC = (0.6 × 12.0%) + (0.4 × 4.5%) = 7.2% + 1.8% = 9.0%.

A project of average firm risk that returns 9% exactly breaks even (NPV = 0); above 9% it creates value, below 9% it destroys value.

Marginal cost and the optimal capital structure

WACC is not constant. As leverage rises, the tax shield initially lowers WACC, but beyond a point the rising costs of financial distress raise both Re and Rd, pushing WACC back up. The trade-off theory predicts a U-shaped WACC curve with a minimum at the optimal debt ratio (see Lesson 7). The marginal cost of capital — the cost of the next dollar raised — can jump at "break points" where the firm exhausts cheap sources (e.g., retained earnings) and must issue new equity with flotation costs.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Book-value weights. Using balance-sheet figures understates equity for profitable firms and distorts WACC. Always use market values.
  • Wrong risk class. Applying the firm-wide WACC to a project of different risk biases decisions — a high-WACC firm will wrongly reject safe projects, a low-WACC firm will wrongly accept risky ones. Use a project- or division-specific ("pure-play") beta instead.
  • Double-counting risk. Adjusting cash flows for risk and raising the discount rate penalizes the project twice.
  • Mismatching the tax shield. Applying (1 − Tc) to equity, or omitting it from debt, is a frequent exam error.
  • Stale inputs. Using the historical coupon as the cost of debt, or an outdated beta, produces a WACC disconnected from current market conditions.

Cross-links

WACC draws on Time Value of Money (Lesson 2) for discounting, on Risk, Return & the CAPM (Lesson 5) for the cost of equity, and feeds directly into Capital Budgeting (Lesson 4) as the hurdle rate. Its sensitivity to the debt mix is the central theme of Capital Structure & Leverage (Lesson 7). Mastering WACC is therefore the hinge between how a firm raises money and how it deploys it.
